name: Krypton
name_origin: From the Greek 'kryptos' meaning 'hidden', reflecting the difficulty of finding this rare gas; named by its co-discoverers William Ramsay and Morris Travers in 1898.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Krypton is extremely rare in Earth's crust but is present in the atmosphere at about 1.14 ppm by volume. It is recovered as a by-product of the cryogenic fractional distillation of liquefied air. Trace amounts are also found in some mineral springs and in the products of nuclear fission.
states: trace gas in Earth's atmosphere (~1.14 ppm)
fission product in spent nuclear fuel

compounds.name: Krypton difluoride
compounds.summary: Volatile colorless solid, the only well-characterized binary krypton compound; an extraordinarily strong oxidizing and fluorinating agent capable of oxidizing gold to AuF5.

compounds.name: Hydridokrypton cyanide
compounds.summary: Matrix-isolated molecule prepared by photolysis of HCN in solid krypton; one of a small family of HKrY species observable only at cryogenic temperatures.

compounds.name: Krypton fluoride hexafluoroarsenate
compounds.summary: Salt containing the [KrF]+ cation; one of the strongest known oxidizers, used in research on high-oxidation-state fluorides.

uses: Fill gas for energy-efficient double- and triple-pane insulating windows
High-performance incandescent and halogen lamp fill (longer life than argon)
Excimer lasers (KrF lasers at 248 nm) for photolithography and eye surgery
Flash lamps for high-speed photography and airport runway approach lights
Tracer for groundwater dating (using the radioisotope 81Kr)
reactivity_summary: Krypton is a colorless, odorless, tasteless noble gas that is chemically very inert. Like the other heavier noble gases, it can be coerced into forming compounds only with the most electronegative partners; its sole stable binary compound is KrF2, which itself is metastable and a powerful oxidizer. Krypton emits a bright white-to-pale-violet glow when excited in a gas-discharge tube.
isotopes_summary: Krypton has six naturally occurring isotopes: 78Kr (~0.36%), 80Kr (~2.29%), 82Kr (~11.59%), 83Kr (~11.50%), 84Kr (~56.99%), and 86Kr (~17.28%); 78Kr is observationally stable but undergoes very slow double electron capture. The longest-lived radioisotope is 81Kr (half-life ~2.3 x 10^5 years), used to date ancient groundwater and ice. 85Kr (half-life ~10.76 years) is a fission product monitored as a tracer of nuclear-reprocessing activity.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Krypton
Greenwood, N. N.; Earnshaw, A. - Chemistry of the Elements, 2nd ed., Butterworth-Heinemann, 1997
